entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: the word they manifest is not parcelled among the locations

entities.name: dhvani
entities.cite: the sounds are many and located

entities.name: deśa
entities.cite: even under the alternative of difference of place

entities.name: kāya
entities.cite: even kayavat things, bodies, stand in place-connections

axioms.name: abheda
axioms.cite: there is still no bheda between dhvani and sabda beyond what the theory already grants

axioms.name: sambandha
axioms.cite: even kayavat things, bodies, stand in place-connections that multiply without multiplying them

axioms.name: uparcarena_sambandha
axioms.cite: spatial predicates attach to the word upacarena, by transfer from its manifestors

denials.name: bheda
denials.cite: the word they manifest is not parcelled among the locations

denials.name: bheda
denials.cite: even kayavat things, bodies, stand in place-connections that multiply without multiplying them

claims.name: abheda
claims.cite: there is no difference between sound and word

claims.name: sambandha
claims.cite: Connection with place and the rest is observed even for embodied things

rejected_readings.label: spatial_parcellation_of_word
rejected_readings.rendering: The eternal word becomes parcelled and differentiated as utterances occur in different places.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the explicit assertion that despite place-difference in sounds (desa-bheda in dhvani), there is no difference in the word itself. The analogy with embodied things shows that location-predicates can be multiple without creating real division.

rejected_readings.label: embodied_thing_localization_error
rejected_readings.rendering: Bodies themselves are differentiated across the multiple places where they occur, and by the same logic, the word too is differentiated across utterance-locations.
rejected_readings.why: Misreads the analogy. The verse's point is to show that embodied things stand in multiple place-connections WITHOUT becoming differentiated or divided—one body in many places remains one body (multiply without multiplying them). This demonstrates that place-multiplicity is compatible with essential non-difference, justifying the denial of difference (bheda) between sound and word despite sounds being multiply-located. Claiming bodies ARE differentiated by place inverts the analogy's structural argument and undermines the parallel reasoning for the word.

notes: The verse establishes non-difference (abheda) between sound and word even under place-difference, paralleling how embodied things stand in multiple locations without division (sambandha without bheda). Core doctrine: spatial predicates attach to the eternal word upacarena (by transfer from manifestors), not constitutive of its own nature. The embodied-thing analogy blocks two distinct mistranslations: direct parcellation of the word by utterance-places, and misreading the analogy as conceding that bodies ARE localized/differentiated.
